paragraphs: South Asian culture expresses itself through ritual, art, and daily life.
Music, dance, and oral traditions carried values across generations.
Craftsmanship and symbolism shaped regional identities.
Festivals and collective memory continue to define culture today.

slides.title: Early Civilizations and Urban Foundations
paragraphs: The Indus Valley Civilization represents one of the earliest urban societies in the world.
Cities such as Harappa and Mohenjo-daro featured grid-based planning, advanced drainage systems, and standardized bricks.
Long-distance trade networks linked South Asia with Mesopotamia, marking early global economic connections.

slides.title: Vedic Culture and Religious Reform
paragraphs: After the decline of Indus cities, Vedic culture shaped early language, rituals, and social structures.
Sacred texts influenced philosophy, law, and education across the region.
Reform movements such as Buddhism and Jainism emerged, emphasizing ethics, compassion, and spiritual balance.

slides.title: Empires, Coinage, and Knowledge Exchange
paragraphs: The Maurya and Gupta Empires unified much of South Asia under organized administration.
These eras promoted coin-based economies, mathematics, astronomy, and medical knowledge.
Buddhism expanded along Silk Road routes, connecting South Asia with Central and East Asia.

slides.title: Medieval Synthesis and Global Trade
paragraphs: Medieval South Asia experienced cultural synthesis through kingdoms, trade, and new religious influences.
Architecture, literature, music, and regional languages flourished across the subcontinent.
The region remained a central hub in Indian Ocean and overland trade networks linking East and West.
